summary: Frazelle reflects on spending a day shadowing investigative journalist Jeff Kao and discovering parallels between journalism and her technical background. She explores how software engineering tools like diffs and version control could serve as mechanisms for transparency and truth-finding in journalism and governance. The post draws connections between text comparison techniques used in investigative pieces (like uncovering fake net neutrality comments) and the everyday tools of source control.
key_points: Investigative journalism shares methodological similarities with academic research, but is driven more by intuition
Diffs and version control could serve as tools for transparency in journalism and law
Text comparison techniques have been used to uncover corporate influence on legislation and fake public comments
Separating emotion from truth in news is a critical challenge in the current media landscape
money_quotes: Humans are individual snowflakes and no one is exactly like the next. It is our unique experiences that form the way we think and behave, as well as what drives us.
Investigative journalism seems to be driven by intuition, while academia might be more driven by novel research.
With a diff, the history of a document is fully transparent, anyone can see any and all changes to it.
So much of the news today is trying to trigger an emotional response for clicks. Or in the worst case, it is trying to trigger an emotional response for influencing an election.
The truth itself should be enough of a trigger.
key_insight: The technical tools engineers use daily for tracking changes and finding differences—like version control diffs—could be powerful instruments for transparency and truth-seeking in journalism and governance.
